schools.summary: The phrase is 'in the word of Jehovah' — the word as a driving power; Jeroboam is caught at the altar, and the prophet's identity is left honestly unknown.
Henry: God countermands the army and sends a prophet in mercy, before Jeroboam's heart hardens; the word is cried to the altar itself.
Gill reviews and rejects the traditional identifications (Iddo, Shemaiah), since this man was soon slain.

debates.question: Who was the man of God out of Judah?

sides.position: unknown and unknowable — conjecture is idle
traditionally Iddo (Jadon), but that is refuted by the chronology

schools.summary: The naming of Josiah, like that of Cyrus in Isa. 44:28, is credible only on a supernatural view of prophecy; the name is significant, not artificial.
Clarke: a most remarkable prophecy, fulfilled 340 years later and attested by two hostile nations.
Henry: nothing future is hidden from God, who named Josiah three centuries before his birth.

debates.question: Are the words 'Josiah by name' original to the prophecy, or a later gloss?

sides.position: possibly a marginal gloss or the chronicler's later insertion
a genuine part of the prediction — foreknowledge extends to names

schools.summary: Keil: the poured-out ashes are a penal sign, marking the desecration of the sacrificial service, not merely the altar.
Gill: the sign confirms the prophet's word and proves him a true prophet of the Lord.
Henry: the sign proves the prophet's mission, shows God's displeasure, reproaches hard hearts, and previews Josiah's ruin of the altar.
The hand 'dried up' by a miraculous withdrawal of vital energy; Jeroboam feels the threatened judgment in his own body.
Henry: the withering both punishes malice against a prophet and restrains the wrath of man; it pictures the sinner hardened past repentance.
Gill: enraged at the saying, the king thrust out his hand to seize the prophet and it withered.
The 'penal miracle,' wrought in the strength of the Lord, takes effect at once and forces the defiant king to entreat the prophet.
Gill: the altar was split into parts, one falling each way, fulfilling the sign to the letter.
Clarke: the altar clave 'of its own accord,' so that the ashes and coals slid off the tilting stones.
Keil: to 'soften the face of the Lord' is to render it soft by intercession; the healing follows at once on the prophet's prayer.
Clarke: 'thy God' betrays that Jeroboam had no portion in the God of Jacob; both miracles aimed to reclaim him.
Henry: the flesh humbled him where the word had not; he sought healing but not pardon, and the prophet returned good for evil.
Gill: the king now answers humbly and supplicating, not haughtily as before.
The invitation is chiefly astute policy: acceptance would look like condonation of Bethel's worship; it reveals a Balak-like, low view of prophecy.
Henry: Jeroboam thanks the prophet but not God; bodily favours make even graceless men grateful to ministers.
Gill: the invitation offers rest and refreshment to a prophet wearied by his journey and prayers.

sides.position: partly genuine gratitude for the healing
astute policy — to buy off the prophet and neutralise his threat

schools.summary: The fast marks God's detestation of idolatry, treating Bethel as excommunicate; Ellicott hears a Balaam-like, ominously emphatic refusal.
Henry: hungry and weary, the prophet obeys rather than reason his way past the command — self-denial that will later be tested.
Gill: the refusal is not contempt of the king but pure obedience to the will of God.
The different road was to prevent delay or detection; Ellicott reads it symbolically — vanish when the work is done — and calls neglect of it the first step to ruin.
Clarke: no bread or water means no communication with idolaters; the reason for the changed road he owns is not easy to see.
Gill: the charge is the command of the Lord by which he came and cried against the altar.

debates.question: Why was the prophet forbidden to return by the way he came?

sides.position: prudence — to avoid delay, questioning, or persecution
to vanish swiftly and leave no trace once his work was done

schools.summary: Gill: in each particular — no food, no drink, a different road — the man of God kept the divine command exactly.

themes: the high point before the fall

schools.summary: Ellicott: a Balaam-like figure — genuine prophetic gifts joined to a worldly temper capable of deceit; he supports idolatry yet clings to the prophetic name.
Henry: a false prophet and bad man, who lost the spirit of prophecy while keeping the name and let his sons frequent Jeroboam's altars.
Clarke: once a true prophet fallen, but not so far as to lose knowledge of the true God — he was not at the king's sacrifice.
Gill: weighs the evidence both ways and declines to settle whether he was a good or a bad man.

debates.question: What manner of man was the old prophet of Bethel?

sides.position: a false prophet and bad man
a genuine but fallen, Balaam-like prophet — true gifts, worldly temper
uncertain — the evidence points both ways

schools.summary: Keil: the sons had marked the road; on their report the old prophet has his ass saddled and rides after the man of God.
Henry: the motive is ambiguous — possibly compassion, but more likely a bad design to snare a man he knew was forbidden to eat there.
Gill: the old prophet asks the way from a strong inclination to see and converse with the man of God.

debates.question: With what intent did the old prophet pursue the man of God?

sides.position: possibly compassion and a desire for acquaintance
a bad design to draw him into a snare

schools.summary: Gill: the old prophet calls for his usual ass, reckoning to overtake on horseback a man travelling on foot.
Keil: the ass saddled, he mounts and rides after the man of God — the pursuit pressed with settled resolve.
The 'oak' or terebinth marks the document's antiquity; Ellicott suspects a needless loitering of the prophet, in keeping with the story's temper.
Clarke: hardly an evil design — the old prophet sought acquaintance and further knowledge of the prophecy, not the man's harm.
Gill: faint, hungry, and thirsty, the man of God rests under the oak, as the ancients sheltered before houses were built.

debates.question: Did the old prophet seek out the man of God with evil intent?

sides.position: no evil design — mere curiosity and desire for acquaintance
the loitering and the story's temper suggest something less innocent

schools.summary: Gill: the invitation seems hearty and without ill design — merely to converse with a man of God about his prophecies at Bethel.
Henry: God's people are more endangered by plausible pretences of sanctity than by open inducements; beware false prophets and try the spirits.
Gill: he refuses to enter the idolatrous city and house, both because the place is unclean and because God forbade it.
Henry: the prophet's own resolution runs together with the divine command — 'I may not, and therefore I will not.'
Gill: persuaded the command came from the Lord himself, the man of God held himself bound to obey it.
Keil: he rehearses to the old prophet the same threefold charge — no bread, no water, no returning by the way he came.

themes: the word of the LORD to me

schools.summary: Clarke: the lie is expressly asserted; yet possibly the old prophet was himself deceived by an angel of darkness masquerading as light.
Ellicott: a gross lie in God's name, believed because it chimed with a secret reluctance to obey — a culpable self-deceit.
Gill: the old prophet affirmed an angel's direction, a claim the man of God had no warrant to trust above God's own word to himself.

debates.question: Was the old prophet's claim a conscious lie or a self-deception?

sides.position: a deliberate falsehood, though he may in turn have been deceived by a dark angel
a gross lie in God's name, believed through culpable self-deceit

schools.summary: Gill: he sinned in trusting another rather than seeking the countermand from the Lord himself; the pleas for him cannot excuse this.
Clarke: even granting God might relax the charge (as with Abraham and Isaac), he should have had it from God's own word, not a stranger's mouth.
Henry: he who withstood a king yielded to a pretended prophet — the plausible pretence of sanctity is the great danger.
Ellicott: the sentence is spoken through the deceiver's own lips at the table of treacherous hospitality — a parable of worldly wisdom beguiling and then condemning.
Clarke, with Kennicott and Josephus: the ambiguous Hebrew allows that God spoke the doom directly from heaven to the true prophet, as to Abraham.
Gill: the old prophet was greatly affected by the word, both for his own guilt and for the man he had seduced.

debates.question: Was the sentence pronounced through the old prophet's mouth or spoken by God directly?

sides.position: through the deceiver's own lips — the most terrible feature of the story
by God directly from heaven to the true prophet, as Josephus and the Arabic assert

schools.summary: Keil: the charge is precise — disobedience to the mouth of the LORD — and the penalty is denial of burial in the fathers' sepulchre.
Gill: the old prophet cries out, pierced by a word that condemns both his guest and his own seducing lie.
Clarke: this is God's own voice from heaven to the man of God, the old prophet having no part in it.

themes: disobedience to the mouth of the LORD

schools.summary: Gill: he kept the charge under the king's greater pressure but broke it under the old prophet's lesser — standing before a throne, falling before a table.
Clarke: the sentence foretells an untimely death without naming its means; the fact is certain, the manner hidden.
Henry: denial of burial with his fathers is a signal disgrace; nothing provokes God like disobedience to an express command, even in a small matter.
Henry: the offence was great and inexcusable; the death magnifies God's honour, warns that office protects none in disobedience, and may serve to harden Jeroboam.
Keil: the meal over, the old prophet saddles the ass for the man he had fetched back, who departs upon it.
Gill: strangely the dread word did not spoil their appetite — they finished the meal before he rode away.
The lion's unnatural restraint marks the death as God's direct act 'according to the word'; Ellicott sets it against the once-common lions of Palestine.
Clarke: the 'sin unto death' — the body punished while mercy reaches the soul; judgment begins at the house of God, severity here being mercy to the spared.
Henry: a real judgment for great disobedience, yet the lion's restraint shows God's anger turned away and the punishment going no further than death.
Gill: a lion, perhaps from the same wood as the she-bears, met and slew him by the way.

debates.question: Why did the true prophet die while the deceiver and the idolatrous king lived?

sides.position: the 'sin unto death' — body judged, soul shown mercy; severity to him was mercy to the unprepared
a judgment for God's glory, yet tempered — his anger turned away, the punishment going no further than death

schools.summary: Gill: the lion neither seized the passing travellers nor deserted the carcass — an unnatural restraint that turns the death into a visible portent.
Ellicott: the old prophet recognises the supernatural fulfilment at once, yet is reticent about his own share — a mixture of remorse and unscrupulous policy.
Gill: the speaker is the very prophet who had deceived him with a lie.
Keil: he finds the corpse and the standing ass untouched by the lion, lifts it onto his ass, and brings it to his city for burial and lament.
Gill: he had more than one ass, having sent the man of God off on one before; he now calls for the one he commonly rode.
Clarke: 'all here was preternatural' — the lion will not devour, the ass will not flee, the lion will not tear the ass; the hand of God is evident.
Keil: the corpse lies cast in the way, guarded by ass and lion, the beast having neither eaten the man nor torn the animal.
Gill: seeing the lion offer no harm to carcass, ass, or passers-by, the old prophet takes up the body without fear.
Gill: the old prophet, unafraid because the lion had harmed neither carcass nor ass nor passers-by, takes up the body and lays it on the ass.
Keil: he lifts the corpse onto his ass and brings it into his own city for burial and mourning.
Clarke: the lament 'Alas, my brother' is simple and pathetic — perhaps grief for the mourner's own guilt, perhaps the customary funeral refrain.
Gill: denied his fathers' tomb, the man of God is laid in the grave the old prophet had prepared for himself — all the honour he could give.
Keil: the burial is attended by the customary Eastern wail, 'hoy achi,' alas, my brother.
Clarke: the request to be buried beside him argues strong conviction that the man was holy, and a wish to share his place in the resurrection.
Keil: the charge flows from the old prophet's certainty that the man of God's prophecy will surely be fulfilled.
Gill: the old prophet solemnly charges his sons to lay his bones beside the man of God's.
Keil: 'cities of Samaria' is the author's proleptic term for the northern kingdom, named in advance from the capital Omri would later build.
Clarke lays out the anachronism and canvasses solutions, including Houbigant's — that God, in prophecy's manner, named Samaria before it existed, as Josiah was named before his birth.
Gill: the saying cried against the altar — that priests should be offered and bones burnt upon it — shall surely be accomplished.

debates.question: How can the verse speak of 'the cities of Samaria' before Samaria was built?

sides.position: a proleptic term inserted by the later narrator, not the prophet
possibly genuine prophecy, naming Samaria in advance as Josiah was named

themes: the word shall surely come to pass

schools.summary: Clarke: idolatry is blinding and infatuating; the miracles moved Jeroboam not at all, and his priesthood, drawn from the lowest, departed from every model.
Ellicott: 'whosoever would, he consecrated' may hint at difficulty finding candidates for the unauthorised priesthood.
Gill: altar rent, hand withered and healed, prophet slain — yet Jeroboam was hardened rather than reformed by it all.
Keil: obstinate persistence in ungodliness is the guilt whose natural consequence is the house's destruction; Ellicott ties the verdict to Ahijah's prophecy and its fulfilment.
Clarke: the abominations were too insulting to endure, so the house was cut off; he digresses into praise of an established national religion.
Gill: 'sin' here stands for its punishment — the persistence that brought evil upon Jeroboam's family.

outliers.note: Turns the verse into an extended defence of the civil establishment of religion, thanking God for the established church of his own country.

themes: sin unto the house of Jeroboam
